questions.q: What is the fundamental difference between class-based and prototype-based OOP?
options: Class-based languages run faster than prototype-based languages
Class-based uses classes as blueprints for objects; prototype-based uses objects as templates for other objects
Prototype-based languages cannot support inheritance
Class-based and prototype-based are synonyms for the same concept
questions.explanation: Class-based languages (Java, C++) create objects from class blueprints, while prototype-based languages (JavaScript, Lua) clone existing objects to create new ones. Both achieve inheritance but through different mechanisms.

questions.q: In JavaScript, how does prototype-based inheritance work?
options: Objects inherit from a parent class defined at compile time
Objects have an internal [[Prototype]] link that chains to other objects, enabling property and method lookup
Each object maintains a list of all available methods
Inheritance in JavaScript is impossible
questions.explanation: JavaScript objects are linked to other objects through the prototype chain. When a property is not found on an object, JavaScript looks up the prototype chain until it finds the property or reaches null.

options: A class that only contains comments
A class that cannot be instantiated and often defines abstract methods that subclasses must implement
A class with no methods
A class that exists only in documentation
questions.explanation: Abstract classes serve as blueprints for subclasses, defining common interface and behavior. They cannot be instantiated directly; subclasses must implement abstract methods and override abstract properties.

questions.q: Why would a language forbid multiple inheritance?
options: To make code execute faster
To avoid the diamond problem and ambiguity when a class inherits from multiple parents with the same method
Because multiple inheritance is always a sign of bad design
Languages that forbid it are inferior to those that allow it
questions.explanation: The diamond problem occurs when a class inherits from two parents that share a common ancestor, creating ambiguity about which version of inherited methods to use. Many languages (Java, C#) avoid this by forbidding multiple inheritance.

questions.q: What are mixins and how do they address the multiple inheritance problem?
options: Mixins are compilation errors that prevent code from running
Mixins are reusable behavior modules that can be combined into classes without the complexity of multiple inheritance
Mixins are the same as classes
Mixins can only be used in languages that support multiple inheritance
questions.explanation: Mixins provide a way to compose behavior from multiple sources by combining standalone behavior modules, avoiding the diamond problem while achieving flexibility similar to multiple inheritance.

questions.q: In a language with abstract classes, what happens if a subclass fails to implement an abstract method?
options: The method is automatically implemented by the parent class
The code will not compile or will raise an error at runtime
The method silently becomes optional
The abstract class becomes concrete
questions.explanation: Abstract methods must be implemented by concrete subclasses. Failing to do so is an error that prevents compilation (in statically typed languages) or raises a runtime error.

questions.q: How can traits or interfaces provide similar functionality to multiple inheritance while avoiding its pitfalls?
options: They don't provide any similar functionality
By allowing classes to implement multiple interfaces with default method implementations, while avoiding the diamond problem
Traits eliminate the need for any inheritance
Interfaces and multiple inheritance are identical concepts
questions.explanation: Interfaces (and traits in languages like Rust and Scala) allow classes to implement multiple contracts with shared behavior while avoiding ambiguity through clear composition rules.

questions.q: Consider a language without explicit class syntax but where objects can clone other objects and override properties. What OOP paradigm is this?
options: Class-based OOP, because all OOP is class-based
Prototype-based OOP, where objects serve as templates for creating new objects
Functional OOP, which has no objects
Imperative OOP, which forbids inheritance
questions.explanation: This describes prototype-based OOP where objects clone and modify existing objects. JavaScript is a primary example of this paradigm.

questions.q: What is the difference between 'abstract' and 'interface' in languages like Java?
options: There is no difference; they are synonyms
Abstract classes can contain state and method implementations; interfaces define a contract with abstract methods only
Interfaces are faster than abstract classes
Abstract classes cannot be inherited from; interfaces can
questions.explanation: Abstract classes can have concrete methods, fields, and constructors; interfaces traditionally define only abstract contracts. Modern languages now allow default method implementations in interfaces, but abstract classes still support state.

questions.q: If a programmer uses 'object composition' in a prototype-based language like JavaScript instead of prototypal delegation, what principle are they following?
options: They are violating JavaScript best practices
They are applying composition over inheritance, favoring delegation of objects over prototype chain lookups
They are forced to do this because JavaScript forbids prototypal inheritance
Object composition and prototypal delegation are identical
questions.explanation: Even in prototype-based languages, composition over inheritance is a valid design principle. Composing behavior through contained objects can be clearer and more flexible than relying on the prototype chain.
